meta.confidence_rationale: After evaluating cost, risk, and feasibility, Option B’s plan→execute pipeline using .sisyphus/plans artifacts provides a stable, well-documented integration path. We are sufficiently confident (≥0.75) that this is viable given the constraints.

chosen_strategy.rationale: Using the plan→execute pipeline is a balanced approach with minimal integration risk, allows structured artifact-based task definition, and easily incorporates our Python orchestrator and eval gating logic. It avoids potential hooking uncertainties in Ralph Loop and the higher development overhead of a full server-based solution.
chosen_strategy.expected_outcome: We will achieve a measurable improvement in experiment throughput under a stable file-based approach that can be iterated easily. Our eval harness can gate or terminate each experiment cycle based on score, and the overhead remains manageable.

rejected_alternatives.reason: Ralph Loop has uncertain support for external hooking and gating with our Python eval harness, introducing unknown integration overhead.
Though flexible, implementing a full server-based integration has a higher engineering cost for building and maintaining the HTTP API client within the 2-week timeframe.
Combining both artifact-based planning and Ralph Loop linking adds complexity and increases the integration surface unnecessarily, elevating risk.

assumptions: OMO’s artifact format (.sisyphus/plans/*.md) remains stable enough for consistent parse and execution.
The Python orchestrator can invoke opencode run commands and gather exit codes or structured logs.
Our eval harness can execute quickly (≤5s/case) and pass a pass/fail signal back to the main loop.

risk_forecast.failure_mode: Incompatible file-based plan format changes in future OMO releases
risk_forecast.detection_signal: Execution fails or OMO logs errors about plan syntax
risk_forecast.mitigation: Pin OMO version; track release notes for changes to plan format

risk_forecast.failure_mode: Eval gating stall (continuous low scores with repeated attempts)
risk_forecast.detection_signal: Multiple attempts never exceed threshold in logs
risk_forecast.mitigation: Implement maximum iteration limits and revert to prior known stable baseline

risk_forecast.failure_mode: Excess overhead from repeated plan expansions
risk_forecast.detection_signal: Longer run times per iteration or memory constraints triggered
risk_forecast.mitigation: Optimize plan structure or reduce concurrency to keep overhead manageable

smallest_validating_experiment.description: Generate a single-step .sisyphus plan, run it via opencode run, and feed its output into the eval harness to confirm it can parse, score, and decide pass/fail automatically.
smallest_validating_experiment.duration: 1 day to set up and validate
smallest_validating_experiment.success_signal: The plan successfully executes, logs a score, and halts or continues based on the threshold; captured in ephemeral logs and local artifact outputs
smallest_validating_experiment.failure_signal: Errors in reading or parsing the plan file, or the harness cannot inject pass/fail gating back to the orchestrator

stop_when: Integration adds >30% overhead to experiment iteration time
New blocking error surfaces in OMO that halts the pipeline for more than 2 days
kill_criteria.pivot_to: Option C (server-based) if local plan execution repeatedly fails or is unsupportable
escalate_when: Repeated failures in hooking up the eval harness or plan parser that remain unresolved after 5 attempts

ordered_tasks.name: Prototype .sisyphus Plan Generation
ordered_tasks.description: Implement a Python method in planner.py to create minimal .sisyphus/plans/*.md from the tasks, including placeholders for each step.
evaluation_criteria: Plans are syntactically valid for opencode run
Plan files exist in .sisyphus/plans/ after generation

ordered_tasks.name: Add Experiment Loop Hook in Python Orchestrator
ordered_tasks.description: In main.py, after each task execution, run the eval harness and determine if the threshold is met or if we should iterate. Store results in ephemeral logs.
evaluation_criteria: Eval harness is triggered immediately after each plan execution
Stop or continue logic is based on the returned score

ordered_tasks.name: Plan→Execute→Evaluate Integration Test
ordered_tasks.description: Execute a basic multi-step plan to confirm the orchestrator calls opencode run. The harness returns a pass/fail signal, gating loop progression.
evaluation_criteria: Successful gating logic: stops if score≥ threshold, or repeats if score< threshold
No unexpected resource overrun or indefinite loops

artifacts_emitted: Runtime logs capturing each iteration’s score
interface_contract.state_updates_for_project_loop: Upon each experiment iteration, the orchestrator updates the next-task queue with pass/fail status. If pass, queue next plan; if fail, iterate again or escalate.
interface_contract.worker_instructions_format: Each .md plan contains steps recognized by opencode run; the Python orchestrator must parse final logs to unify with the lab’s result store.

memory_actions.value: We learned that .sisyphus/plans/*.md with short, discrete steps is reliable for incremental gating with a Python orchestrator.

verification.how_to_confirm_this_was_right: Compare the experiment throughput and success rates before and after integration using the harness output. Observe if iteration gating is functioning properly and we see a cost under $0.10 per iteration.
verification.observable_result: A gradually improving eval score (0.562 → more) with stable overhead, no indefinite loops or major errors.
reversal_conditions: Plan parsing becomes incompatible or unmaintainable
Continuous integration failures persist across multiple expansions of plan tasks
